name: Retry amplification in call chains
trigger_shapes: retry loop around an external API call
retry decorator or policy applied at multiple layers of the call stack
microservice calling microservice with retries at each hop
SDK or client with built-in retry on top of application-level retry
activation: A transient failure in a downstream service (e.g., timeout or 503) triggers retry logic at the immediate caller. If callers upstream of that caller also have retry logic, and the operation is not idempotent end-to-end, each retry fans out into further retries.
mechanism: Downstream service fails with a timeout; immediate caller retries 3x (standard policy); each retry arrives at the downstream service as a new request; if a layer above the immediate caller also has retries, it retries the already-retrying caller; total load on the downstream service becomes (original_requests x retries_layer_1 x retries_layer_2 x ...). With 3 retries at 3 layers, a single request becomes 27 requests. Resource exhaustion cascades laterally to healthy services sharing infrastructure.

signal_sequence.signal: Overall request rate appears normal or only slightly elevated. Success metrics mask underlying failures because some retries succeed.
Retry count metrics rise in logs. Latency p99 creeps up. Error rates in downstream service increase but stay below alert thresholds.
Latency spikes spread across services. Thread pools and connection pools in upstream services begin exhausting. CPU/memory anomalies in services that are not the root cause.
Widespread timeouts and errors. All services degraded even though the original failure was isolated to one downstream dependency.

threshold_estimated: 50% error rate sustained for 10 seconds in the downstream service. At that rate, without circuit breakers, retry amplification reaches runaway load within 1-2 retry cycles. Based on common circuit breaker defaults in AWS boto3 and Hystrix.
invisible_because: Each individual service's retry policy looks reasonable in isolation (3 retries is standard). No end-to-end load simulation tests the multiplicative effect. Per-service dashboards show retry counts rising but not the cross-service fan-out multiplier. Success metrics from retries mask that the system is working much harder than traffic levels suggest.
diagnostic: Does distributed tracing show a retry fan-out multiplier greater than 1 across more than 2 layers in simulated failure scenarios? Specifically: trigger a single downstream failure and count how many total requests arrive at the downstream service.
countermeasures: Implement circuit breakers at each service boundary — open the circuit after threshold failures to stop retry fan-out at the source.
Add jitter to all retry delays to prevent synchronized retry storms across service instances.
Enforce a retry budget at the edge (e.g., one retry total per user-facing request) and make all internal hops fail-fast without retrying.
Use idempotency keys so retries do not duplicate side effects, enabling safe pass-through without fan-out.
Add end-to-end load simulation tests that inject downstream failures and measure total request amplification at the leaf service.

sources.title: AWS US-EAST-1 Outage Postmortem — retry storms at 3 retries without jitter
Temporal.io — Error Handling and Compensation in Distributed Systems

misdiagnosis: Downstream service needs more capacity — team scales horizontally; retry storm follows the new instances at the same amplification ratio
Network instability — team investigates network metrics instead of the per-service retry multiplier; root cause stays hidden
Downstream service has a bug — team focuses on fixing the downstream without first capping the inbound retry load that is preventing recovery

recovery_cost.time_with_card: 5-10 minutes (disable retries via feature flag, load drops immediately)
recovery_cost.blast_radius: all users if retry amplification reaches the edge service; service-wide 500s
